[music]
Hello everyone. I hope you're all having
a great time. My name is Abishek Virala.
I'm the founder of Aqua.sh which is a
DevOps and cloud consulting company.
People also know me through my YouTube
channel where I create a lot of content
in the space of DevOps, cloud and
artificial intelligence.
Over the last 3 years, we built an
amazing community of close to 1 million
followers across different social media
platforms.
Apart from all these, I'm also into
software engineering. I worked as a
software engineer for 11 years. The last
company that I worked for was Red Hat
where I led a team that focused on
security as well as opensource.
This is a brief introduction about
myself. Before we get started with the
topic, I would like to thank Sona team
for providing me this wonderful
opportunity today. Okay. Now, let me
start today's session by asking you all
a very interesting question.
Have you ever thought in the world of AI
assisted software engineering when AI
writes the code who owns the bug?
This is a very important question to ask
because we are moving towards the
biggest structural shift in engineering.
We are witnessing the fastest workflow
transformation in the history where
developers are using artificial
intelligence for every single activity
that they perform.
It can be for building a complete new
application or maybe adding a new
feature to the existing application or
even fixing issues with the existing
features. In a way, this is good because
AI is enhancing the developer
productivity. But what about the bugs
that are introduced by AI generated
code? Now, one thing is clear, AI
generated code is no exception. It also
introduced bugs most importantly
security related bugs. So who takes the
ownership of these bugs? How do you
identify these bugs and mitigate them
before going to the production? And most
importantly, is it possible to automate
this workflow? The workflow of
identifying bugs in the AI generated
code and fixing them before going to the
production.
This is exactly what I will talk in
today's session. I also have a demo
towards the end. Please try to watch it
as well. Cool. So I'll start the session
by showing you a very important
statistic.
This is from GitHub October analysis.
As per this analysis, 46%age of the
repos that are being created today are
actually AI assisted repositories.
Either developers are using AI agents to
create the repositories completely or
they are using AI assistants to generate
the code locally and then push the code
to the repositories. In any of these
cases, this number is a very strong
indicator to tell us AI is enhancing the
developers productivity. And today
developers are using AI to generate
large volumes of the code.
It also comes with a downside which is
what I want to focus. The downside is
that because of the A assisted
development the review cycles have
significantly shortened.
Let's take a simple example here.
Imagine a development team a small
development team with only two
developers.
Previously before AI, let's say
developer A creates two pull requests
per week. The other developer acts as a
reviewer and reviews two pull requests
and because only two pull requests,
developer B can review them quite
thoroughly. But now with A assisted
development, developer A can create
let's say 7 to eight pull requests a
week. That means within the same time
frame that is within one week time
frame. Now developer B has to review
seven to eight pull requests.
Now this actually creates a burden on
the reviewers and because of this the
reviewers have gradually developed a
bias that AI generated code is usually
good. So let's only review the
functional part of the code. Let's
verify if the functional part and if the
logic looks good and then let's review
and merge the code. This way they can
review seven to eight pull requests in a
week. But this creates a problem. The
functional part of the application is
fine but the AI generated code
introduced security vulnerabilities in
the application and this application
goes to production and you all know what
happens when there are security
vulnerabilities in the production. It
becomes a gateway for the hackers.
This is exactly what is called as
security paradox of AI code.
This is a wonderful picture that exactly
depicts what's happening. Reviewers are
only looking at the surface level. By
surface level, I mean reviewers check if
the code compiles well. They verify if
the code runs well and they only look at
the functional test cases. If they are
passing, then go ahead and merge the PR.
But AI generated code as I told you it
comes with lot of hidden patterns and
insecure implementations which is
beneath the surface of the code.
hidden patterns like outdated packages
or deprecated patterns that introduce
certain risk in the application
and also insecure implementations
something like SQL injection or SSRF
that can be embedded into functionally
correct logic.
The nutshell or the bottom line here is
that AI generated code can look good at
the surface level because it can be
compiled. It works fine. The functional
test cases are also passing but
underneath they come with hidden
patterns and insecure implementation
that leads to security vulnerabilities
in the application and as the
application goes to production it is
easy gateway for the hackers.
Now the big question is how to eliminate
this how to avoid issues with AI
generated code.
Now this should be a practice.
There are set of steps that organization
should follow. Number one,
organizations should completely
eliminate the trust bias.
Today as I told you a lot of developers
consider AI generated code. They blindly
test it. They consider it is good.
Similarly reviewers also they only look
at the logic and if the logic is clean,
they merge the code. This should be
eliminated. AI generated code should be
treated the same way as human generated
code. There should not be any additional
trust on the AI generated code. Along
with that, organization should also
define security baselines.
I personally believe every organization
should enforce a gold standard security
baseline.
What is a gold standard security
baseline? So this should not be optional
set of best practices or suggestions to
try to be careful but this should be set
of tools or set of practices, set of
checks that should be introduced even
before the code goes to peer review
process. Something like adding checks or
some tools in the code review process.
even before the code is reviewed by the
peer reviewer.
And there should be four pillars for
this gold standard.
Whether it is tools or whether it is
some practices that you add to your CI
pipeline, it should be consistent.
Again, whether it is a human or whether
it is AI that wrote the code and create
the pull request.
The same rule should apply to all the
authors
and also it should be automated.
One should not expect the reviewer to
run these checks or to run the tools and
then to merge the pull request but
instead this entire thing should be
executed as part of the pull request
and the tools that you choose. This is
very very important when it comes to AI.
Let's say you introduce a tool as part
of the pull request process to verify AI
generated code does not have any
security issues. The tool that you use
should be completely security aware and
also it should be context aware. It
should understand the real
vulnerabilities, latest vulnerabilities
in the world and it should not just look
at the syntax or semantics of the code.
Finally, this entire thing should follow
the shift left approach. As I told you,
you know, it should not be verified once
the application goes to production, but
instead as the pull request is created,
the gold standard should be executed
first. The tool that you introduce in
your organization or the script that you
introduce, it should be executed first
and only then the peer review process
should follow. This is called the shift
left approach.
And finally, it is also important to
treat AI as untrusted input. Very
simple. Just like how you treat an
external contractor. Let's say your
organization has an external contractor
and this person submits a pull request.
You would be extra cautious with the
pull request. You wouldn't inherently
trust the pull request or the changes.
Fine. Now you must be thinking but how
to introduce this culture within the
organization. How to eliminate trust
bias? How to introduce gold standard and
how to treat AI as untrusted input. This
is exactly where dev secops comes into
picture. You must have seen the gaining
popularity of dev secops. The reason is
simple. AI generated code is introducing
a lot of security vulnerabilities in the
applications.
So this is how a modern dev sec ops
workflow would look like.
Developers works on code generation.
They will continue to use AI assistance
to generate the code. Dev sec ops
instance would introduce automated
scanning into the workflow. Developers
can create commits. They can create pull
requests. They would pass through
automated scanning. Maybe dev sec ops
engineer would introduce a tool. That
tool will not only look at the surface
level but also look at underlying
vulnerabilities and insecure patterns in
the code. And finally, dev secops
engineers would also set up quality gate
feedback. This is the most important
thing. Equality gate feedback is
something like
block the commit if there are more than
two low secure vulnerabilities or block
the commit if there is one high insecure
vulnerability. So this is how within the
workflow dev sec ops can set up a
quality gate. If the quality gate is a
failure then the commit or the pull
request should be blocked. So the dev
sec workflow would introduce SEA, SAS
and quality gates. SEA is basically
software composition analysis which will
look at the dependencies within the
application and verify if any dependency
is using outdated version. SAS which is
basically to look at code patterns to
see if there is anything like SQL
injection or any other insecure
patterns. quality gets to enforce or to
provide go or no go signals on the pull
requests or the commits. As someone who
has worked on security in the past, if
you want to automate these things, it
can be sea, it can be sashed or it can
be quality gates, I would recommend
sonar cube cloud because sonar cube
cloud comes with native CI/CD
integration.
You can integrate this with your GitHub,
Bitbucket, wherever you have your source
code. As developer creates a commit or a
pull request, the quality gates along
with SCA as well as SAS would be
executed by Sonar Cube cloud. And Sonar
Cube cloud is always updated with
security rules reflecting the latest
vulnerability landscape. So you don't
have to worry if there is a new
vulnerability maybe that was introduced
last week. Sonar Cube cloud is already
updated with the information. It also
comes with multi- language support.
It comes with 30 plus language support.
So if you're using Java, Python, NodeJS
or any other programming languages, you
don't have to worry. You can stick to
one dev sec ops platform that is Sonar
Cube cloud. On top of that, it also
provides actionable feedback. So it can
help you understand what exactly is the
vulnerability and how you can your
development team or dev sec ops team can
address [clears throat] the
vulnerability and fix that in real time.
And on the sonar cube platform which
we'll look at during the demo, you can
actually map your risks. So you can
understand because sonar categorize
risks into actionable vulnerabilities
and points for human reviews. So you'll
find different sections like the code
smell section, security hotspot section
and vulnerabilities section. So it
becomes easy for your team as well as
organization to manage all the security
related issues. Perfect. Now it's time
for the demo. Let's see sonar cube cloud
in action. Okay. So for the purpose of
demo I used GitHub copilot I used the
claude opus 4.6 model and I created a
Python task manager. So this application
is completely AI generated application
and I also verified the application is
working fine. So I'm logged into the
application as the admin user. I'm able
to add some tasks. I'm able to look at
the pending task and I can also mark the
task as star. So what I'm trying to say
here application is working functionally
fine. But let's scan this application
using sonar cloud and let's see what is
the status of the security scanning. Are
there any security vulnerabilities with
this application? How to do it? So first
we need an account with sonar cloud.
Just head to sonarsource.com.
Click on get started and sign up with
any of the authentication providers. I
have my application source code hosted
on GitHub. Sign up with GitHub. That's
all. It's one single step. And I have my
account set up with Sonar Cloud. So to
scan the Python task manager
application, I'll click on analyze new
project. Because the GitHub integration
is already set up, I can choose the
organization within my GitHub and scroll
down to choose the repository that I
want to scan. In my case, Python task
manager.
Click on the setup and define what is
new code. This is the important part.
When you're dealing with Sonar Cloud,
you are provided with the option for new
code definition. For example, do you
want to perform scanning on every new
commit or every new pull request? Then
you can go for previous version option.
Or let's say you want to run the
scanning on last few days modified code.
Let's say you want to run the scanning
on last 7 days code. Then you can define
number of days and choose the dates. I
would recommend previous version as an
option. So I'll select previous version
and click on create project.
That's all. Now your Python task manager
code that you have on GitHub is set up
with Sonar Cloud. Every time you make a
commit, every time you make a pull
request,
you will see the scanning report on
Sonar Cloud. also for the first time
because you have set up your Python task
manager with sonar cloud it will show
you the initial report that is what is
the current state of your Python task
manager application. There you go. So it
says there are 15 open issues. This is
what I was talking about. Functionally
the application is working as expected
but AI generated code introduced
security vulnerabilities within the
application. Now imagine I make this
application production ready. It is a
gateway for the hackers because there
are 15 vulnerabilities out of which 14
vulnerabilities are blockers which needs
immediate attention. This is the
application is not ready for the
production. I'll also show you how this
works. I mean how the automated scanning
works when we create a pull request. But
let me quickly go through the analysis
for you. So you can look at the
vulnerabilities according to the
severity. There are 14 blockers and one

want to understand what exactly does one

to non-construct SQL query. By looking

click on it where I can find the
analysis where is this issue
why this is an issue how can I fix this
very important like it also provides the
code change that is required which is
very helpful for the developers they can
see what exactly is the problem they can
see what code change should be done they
can open it in the ID and they can make
the required change and raise the pull
request. test. You can also see the

comments, you can see what exactly is
the status. Now let's verify by creating

and let's say I update the flash

That's why so analysis says this pull

Recently Sonar cloud added AI code
